title: Bond ETF Expense Ratios Compared: BND vs IUSB vs AGG vs SCHZ
description: Comparing 2026 expense ratios for total bond market ETFs, government bond ETFs, and corporate bond ETFs. Which one is the absolute cheapest? Check out our cost breakdown to find out.

mainEntity.name: What is the BND expense ratio for 2026?

acceptedAnswer.text: As of early 2026, the Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND) has an official expense ratio of 0.03%. This makes it extremely competitive among total bond market funds.

mainEntity.name: Is the IUSB expense ratio competitive with AGG and BND?

acceptedAnswer.text: The iShares Core Universal USD Bond ETF (IUSB) has an expense ratio of 0.06%. While slightly higher than BND and AGG (both at 0.03%), IUSB includes a broader spectrum of bonds, including high-yield debt, which generally carries slightly higher management costs.

mainEntity.name: What is the Vanguard BND expense ratio official 2026 figure?

acceptedAnswer.text: The official 2026 expense ratio for the Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND) is 0.03%. Vanguard is known for maintaining some of the lowest fees in the industry.

mainEntity.name: Are there cheaper bond ETFs than BND?

acceptedAnswer.text: Several total bond market ETFs, including AGG (iShares) and SCHZ (Schwab), match BND's 0.03% expense ratio. It is rare to find broad total bond market ETFs lower than 0.03%, but some short-term or specific Treasury ETFs (like VGSH or VGIT) also hit that bottom-tier pricing of 0.03%.

mainEntity.name: Why does SPLG ETF expense ratio 2026 matter for bond portfolios?

acceptedAnswer.text: While SPLG (SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF) is an equity fund, investors often pair ultra-low-cost stock funds like SPLG (0.02% expense ratio) with ultra-low-cost bond funds like SPAB (0.03%) or BND to build an incredibly cheap two-fund core portfolio.
